q.content: What is the Universe History Well-being Level? How is it defined?
q.explanation: The "UHWL" is the sum total of the Lifetime Well-being Levels ("LWLs") of every being that exists, has existed, and ever will exist in the entire history of the universe.
q.study: If the UHWL is positive, does that mean the number of beings in the history of the universe with a positive LWL is greater than the number with a negative LWL? Why or why not?

q.explanation: Utilitarianism says that an action is objectively morally wrong if and only if there is something else you could do that would result in a higher Universe History Well-being Level.
q.study: Does Utilitarianism always tell us to benefit as many people as possible? Why or why not?

q.content: What will Utilitarianism say if there are two or more actions that would each result in the same UHWL, but no other action would result in higher UHWL than them?
q.explanation: Utilitarianism says it’s permissible to do any of those two or more actions.

q.content: What will Utilitarianism say if all of your options would result in negative UHWL, but some would result in less negative UHWL than others?
q.explanation: Utilitarianism will say to perform an option that will result in the least negative UHWL compared with the alternatives.

q.content: What does the "No Guidance" objection to Utilitarianism say?
q.explanation: It says this: *It seems that a moral theory should be useful. It should give us real-world guidance. But Utilitarianism seems like it will typically be useless, since we typically can’t know what action would result in the highest UHWL.*
q.study: What are the two responses to this objection that a utilitarian could make that we discussed in class?

q.content: What is the "Best Intentions" objection to Utilitarianism?
q.explanation: The Best Intentions objection says: *If Utilitarianism is true, then failing to raise the UHWL as much as you can is objectively immoral. But no! An act is not wrong when the person doing it can't predict the outcome and is acting with good intent. So, Utilitarianism is false.*
q.study: What is the response to this discussed in class? (Hint: wrong versus blame...)

q.content: What does a "Rights Theorist" in ethics believe?
q.explanation:
Rights Theorists claim that there are certain rights that all people have simply because they are people. These rights are known as *moral rights* (sometimes they are called *human* rights).
Moral rights are supposed to be shared by all people; they are not dependent on the laws of any government and cannot be taken away by any government.
Rights Theorists disagree about exactly which rights are moral rights, but they mostly agree that our moral rights include the right to life, the right to liberty, the right to fair and equal treatment by governments, and the right to not be held in slavery or servitude.
q.study: A famous Rights Theorist document outlining a number of supposed moral rights is the UN Human Rights Declaration, which you can find [here](https://www.un.org/en/about-us/universal-declaration-of-human-rights).

q.content: What is the Angry Mob case and how is it used to challenge Utilitarianism using moral rights?
q.explanation:
In the Angry Mob case someone has killed the town’s beloved schoolteacher. The townspeople all think that Jones did it. Jones is hiding in the sheriff's office. Some people in the town have formed a mob.
If the sheriff shoots Jones and hands over the body, the mob will disperse. If the sheriff lets the mob have Jones, they will tear him to pieces---a much more painful way to die. If the sheriff protects Jones, the mob will riot, several innocents (not members of the mob) will be killed, and many will be badly injured.
Utilitarianism says the sheriff must shoot Jones, since this both prevents the riot and minimizes Jones's suffering. But many intuitively judge that the morally correct thing to do is to protect Jones, as this preserves Jones's rights, even though the UHWL will be lower.

q.content: What is the Utility Monster objection to Utilitarianism?
q.explanation:
You have five thousand pounds of rice you don’t need.
You could give one pound each to five thousand starving people, which would result in +500,000 well-being added to the universe.
You could also give all five thousand pounds to the Utility Monster, who will experience a +1,000,000 boost in well-being.
Utilitarianism says the objectively morally correct thing to do is to give the rice to the monster, since this will add more to the UHWL.
But to many it seems Utilitarianism's verdict is clearly wrong, since the intuitively moral thing to do is to feed the villagers.

q.content:
Utilitarianism says that often the only morally acceptable lifestyle for someone in the developed world is to donate all their discretionary income --- what's left after taxes and basic necessities are paid for --- to an effective charity.
*Why does it say this?*
q.explanation:
Often, the more needy a person is, the easier it is to raise their well-being level. (For example, a sandwich to a starving man is a huge boost of well-being, but not much of a boost to someone well-fed and not hungry. Another example: 50 cents means almost nothing to a typical American, but to a child in a very poor nation, it can purchase a deworming pill that will cure and prevent intestinal parasites for 6 months.)
Effective charities can therefore often create far more well-being per dollar spent compared to what a typical American creates when spending on themselves. Utilitarianism tells us we must maximize the well-being we create with our choices. This implies we must often spend all non-essential money we make on effective charities.
q.study: To many this is a reason to reject Utilitarianism as "too demanding". But Utilitarians challenge us to explain the difference between a child suffering in a developing country and a child dying right in front to us. To see how effective small donations can be, visit [this site](https://www.givewell.org/) or [this site](https://www.evidenceaction.org/).

q.content: What is the Drowning Baby argument?
q.explanation:
P1) Common sense morality says it IS WRONG ignore a drowning baby in front of you just to avoid ruining new shoes.
P2) Common sense morality says it IS NOT WRONG to ignore a starving child just to spend money on something besides charity.
P3) There is no real morally important difference between these two children.
C) Therefore, common sense morality is *inconsistent* and should be discarded in favor of a consistent moral theory like Utilitarianism, which says we have a moral duty to both the drowning baby and the starving child.
q.study: To see how effective small donations can be, visit [this site](https://www.givewell.org/) or [this site](https://www.evidenceaction.org/).
